questions.scenario: A release passed CI and staging. The release record says staging ran image digest `sha256:9c1c...7f54`, but the production workflow builds a new image from `main` at deploy time and pushes the tag `orders-api:latest`. What is the core release risk?

options.text: Production may run different bytes than staging tested, because the workflow rebuilt instead of promoting the recorded digest.
The production deploy will always fail because ECS cannot run images built by GitHub Actions.
The release is safe as long as the tag name is the same in staging and production.
The only risk is that the Docker build takes longer than the staging deploy.

questions.explanation: The article's promotion rule is build once, promote one immutable artifact. A tag can move, and a rebuild can produce different bytes. Production should deploy the digest that staging tested. Matching tag names do not prove matching image content.

questions.scenario:
The production gate receives this staging evidence before approval:

```text
/version: version=1.8.4 commit=8f3a12c6 imageDigest=sha256:9c1c...7f54
/readyz: 200 ready=true
/smoke/checkout: accepted orderId=test-442
release record image digest: sha256:9c1c...7f54
```

What does this evidence prove best?

options.text: Staging ran the release record digest and the smallest checkout path worked there.
Production will never fail after approval.
The production secrets are correct because staging returned 200.
The checkout API has no possible data compatibility risk.

questions.explanation: The evidence proves the release artifact identity and a narrow staging behavior check. It does not prove production secrets, full production traffic behavior, or every data compatibility case.

questions.scenario: A workflow has `deploy-production` with `needs: build`, but not `needs: deploy-staging`. The staging job can fail while production still starts after build. Which review comment is most accurate?

options.text: Production should depend on the staging gate so a failed staging deploy stops the release before users are affected.
Production should run first because production has more realistic traffic.
The dependency does not matter if both jobs use the same runner image.
The staging job should be removed because approvals are enough.

questions.explanation: The promotion path is build, deploy staging, check staging, then production approval and deploy. If production only depends on build, the staging gate cannot block it. Approval is a separate gate, not a replacement for staging evidence.

questions.scenario: The staging job and production job both use `environment: production` because the team wants both jobs to read the same AWS role. Why is this a bad design?

options.text: Staging should use production credentials only when the app is stateless.
Staging and production have different blast radius and should receive separate environment-scoped access.
GitHub Actions environments cannot be used with deployment jobs.
The staging job will run slower because production environments require more CPU.

questions.explanation: The article separates staging and production because they touch different data and have different consequences. Environment-scoped secrets and roles keep staging access away from production credentials. This is about blast radius, not runner performance.

questions.scenario:
A release manager approves production after seeing this staging check:

```text
/readyz: 200
/version: version=1.8.4 commit=8f3a12c6 imageDigest=sha256:aaaa
release record image digest: sha256:bbbb
```

What should happen next?

options.text: Continue, because `/readyz` returned 200.
Stop the promotion because staging is not running the digest recorded for this release.
Deploy production with the digest from staging and update the release record later.
Skip the version check because commit is more important than image digest.

questions.explanation: The digest mismatch breaks the build once, promote once chain. `/readyz` proves the service is ready, but it does not prove the right artifact is being promoted. The release record should remain the source of truth.

questions.scenario: A production deployment job for a checkout API is being reviewed. Which changes strengthen the release gate design?

options.text: Use a production environment with required reviewers before production credentials are available.
Add concurrency so only one production deployment for `orders-api` can run at a time.
Let production rebuild from the default branch so it always has the newest code.
Require production to depend on a successful staging deploy and smoke test.
Store the rollback task definition in the release record before production changes.

questions.explanation: Required reviewers, concurrency, staging dependency, and a named rollback target all support controlled promotion. Rebuilding from the default branch weakens the artifact chain because production may not run what staging tested.
